text: As mobile developers, one of the most empowering feelings is taking an idea and turning it into a tangible app
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: your friends and your family can use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For many of us, this inspiration was the reason we became Android developers, like the ability to just imagine an app and make it real.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you wake up brimming with enthusiasm, you grab your coffee and you open up Android Studio.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But before you write a single line of code, you're hit with an avalanche of decisions.

text: Should I use Jetpack Compose?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay, sure.

text: But which version?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Which libraries?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do I use Material Design 3?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Should I go with a single activity or multiple composable screens?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Or maybe stick to fragments?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Yuck.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then you think, Ugh, okay, I need to hit an API for this idea.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Should I use Retrofit?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: OKHTTP?

text: Maybe KTAR?

text: I actually need a JSON serialization library here.

text: Maybe I should add Timber.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: First, Gradle version catalog.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is the default standard, by the way, so that shouldn't come as a surprise.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So dependencies, SDK versions, min SDK, target SDK, compile SDK, even the app's namespace, the version code, the version name, I just put all of them in my version catalog.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The second thing, Gradle convention plugins.

text: If you have more than one Android library module in your app, don't you find it frustrating that you have to repeat the whole thing?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The JVM target, the plugins, KSP if you're using it, the library dependencies, build target, all of those things, Kotlin options.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I have these plugins, these custom plugins set up so you can literally drop one line at the top of your build Gradle file and you can just start writing code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it reduces so much of the boilerplate that you otherwise have to write.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'll be honest with you, some of this was an absolute pain in the ass to set up, but I have it locked down now and hopefully I never have to figure this out again.

text: You know, I'm actually surprised more people don't use this.

text: I use this especially for running very specific tasks while I'm building an app.
